cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript exactly as written. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to refer back to the passage provided. The passage contrasts novels with plays and screenplays. It mentions that the novelist has absolute control over the text until it's published. The relevant part says, "The novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience. He may be advised by his editor to revise his text, but if the writer refuses to meet this condition no one would be surprised. It is not unknown for a well-established novelist to deliver his or her manuscript and expect the publisher to print it exactly as written." So the key point here is the absolute control the novelist has over the text. The answer choice A says "Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text." That seems to directly reference the passage. Let me check the other options. Option B talks about the paperback being portable and adaptable. The passage does mention that as a positive aspect of novels, but that's more about their convenience and accessibility, not about the control over the published text. So probably not B. Option C says the novel is limited to a single channel of information—writing. This is a reason why the novel can go anywhere in terms of narrative, but does it explain why the publisher would print it as is? Maybe not directly. The single channel aspect is about the medium, but the question is about the author's control. So maybe C isn't the answer. Option D says the novelist is seldom advised by editors to revise. But
